speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 16, 1998. Chaim Engel is being interviewed in Branford, Connecticut, United States by Betsy Stern in English. Today is October 16, 1998. I am Betsy Stern interviewing Chaim Engel in Branford, Connecticut, United States, in English. Let's begin, Mr. Engel, by your introducing yourself. Tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Chaim Engel. I am born in Poland in January the 10th, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name is Brudzew, a small, very small town. B-r-u-d-z-e-w.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Samuel and my mother was Frieda. I had one brother. His name is-- was Meier. He was nine years younger from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He started-- when he married, he started-- he worked in textile. And they lived in a small, very small place. And because he had a business, they called him 'the Millionaire.' Now, he probably just made a living on this business. But anyway, because the kind of work he did in a business, he was called 'Millionaire.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Called 'Millionaire' by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the not-Jewish population. So he got fed up with and he decided to go away. I was, I think, 5 years old. And I was 5 years when we moved to Lodz. And the rest of the years we lived in Lodz. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what life was like at all before you moved to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was 5 years old. I don't remember much about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- did your parents have family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father's brothers and sisters, and my mother had one brother-- one brother that I knew of it. Maybe were some before what I didn't know. Anyway, and-- was a-- a quiet life, a pleasant life. And as I say, my father decided to go away there. And he moved-- He was really-- by trade, he was a finished rabbi, although he didn't practice as a rabbi. But he was really a Talmudic scholar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he gotten his ordination? Had he gotten his semikhah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I don't know that, but I know he studied for it. But as I say, he didn't practice it. And-- but when he went to-- to Lodz, he started to go in business but was not successful, and a lot of misfortunes. And after this, he couldn't make it in-- in the business, he went to be-- be a-- a teacher, a Hebrew teacher. And that is what he did most of the years. I think he-- we made a living, but was not a rich living. It was a very modest living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some schools, he-- Jewish schools where they teach Hebrew. It was a mixture school, Hebrew and secular [INAUDIBLE] studies. So he teach Hebrew studies. Matter of fact, I went also to the same schools. So I had my regular education and my Hebrew education there. And-- and my mother worked too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know she maybe-- I don't remember exactly. We-- something-- sewing or something like that. And-- though it was not an easy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you live in a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really, especial-- you didn't have any like a ghetto, something like that. That was not in that time. But you-- Jews mostly lived next to the other Jews, but not exclusive Jewish. But a more Jewish. There was a synagogue not far and things like that. So it was, I would say, a Jewish neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father was. Yeah, my father was, as I said, religious. Not fanatic religious, but we had an observant, Jewish observant, household. So my mother light the candles, and my father went to shul. Things like that. So I think I would say it was a religious upbringing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to shul with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I go. I went with him till later when I started to, like a teenager, started to rebel. I didn't go so much more to shul. I-- I had always payos and things like that. When I was about 13, I gave up on that. I didn't do that. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father upset when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he probably didn't say it, but I assume he was upset about. Look, and-- any teenager which rebels against their parents, surely the parents are upset about it. I assume. He never told me that, but I assume. And later, when I finish my school-- my school was-- I would think and I'd compare it to like two years college here, maybe. And then I want-- I was working. An uncle of mine had a stocking factory, and I worked with him there for a short time till I was 19. I had to go-- in Poland, we had mandatory service. I had to go in the army. I went in the Polish army. Anyways, I was an infantryman. And you go for 1 and 1/2 year to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a minute. Before we talk about the army, I know that you-- you lived with your father, your mother, your brother. Your brother went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was, yeah, nine years younger. So when I almost was finished with everything, so he started probably to go to school then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died in 1936. She was got sick-- bronchitis or whatever, I don't know. Anyway, she died. And that was in 1936. And later, my father remarried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that when you were in the army, that your father-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, before that. It must be before that, because-- yeah, that was before that, I think. Yep. That was before that. And he remarried. And I didn't like the person he married. But I was already half foot out of the house, so-- although I lived at home, but I was not so more involved with the house anymore. And-- and he remarried, I say, and-- and I went-- I went to the army. Well, I don't know exactly what happened between. But anyway, I went to the army. And I was supposed to be finished with my service on September the 15th, 1939. But the war broke out in Poland on the 1st, September the 1st of 1939. So I was an active soldier in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Germany attacked, while you were in the army, how did the Gentile Poles treat the Jewish soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, although I didn't have any direct experience, bad experience with not-Jewish, but there was, for example, sections in Lodz where I lived where I wouldn't go as a Jew there because the anti-Semitism was in there. And I was told, don't go there because there is not a Jew goes there, things like that. Just because that was strict not-Jewish area, and there were very big anti-Semites. But I really cannot remember that I have some bad experience by myself-- by myself. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends who weren't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. All my friends were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went in-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went into the army, did you become friendly with anybody who wasn't Jewish in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really friend. But I would assume-- we were together in these different mixtures of-- you had some people which you liked more than others. But not a special friendship with, because the circumstances didn't bring it to be more friendly with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't remember any special-- I know a few friends what I had, they were Jewish. Yeah. That's what I say. But not really a-- a close-- close connection with not-Jewish. I didn't have, really. Was it a problem for you, being a Jew in the Polish army and trying to-- were you trying to be observant in any kind of way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not observant. And I didn't have any problems with being just a Jew. But you always kept far away, because the air-- atmosphere was very anti-Semitic. Matter of fact, right on the front, I was more afraid for the Polish citizens and the Polish friends than for the Germans, because you were afraid. You had a reservation about not to be too close with them because you didn't trust them. Didn't trust them.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were your fellow soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. So-- so we-- we'd be so-- a danger more that they can shoot you. They're not the same Polish soldiers what would go together, fight for the Germans. They were-- you have to watch them, too. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- I'm sorry, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on September 1?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the war broke out, and we always had to go in the front. And the war lasted for three weeks. So it was very disorganized. They had the [INAUDIBLE] and the star. At last, we ended up being on the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the [INAUDIBLE] was in the front, and the Germans-- they came to stay in the front of the Germans. A matter of fact, I never understand that. When I was in the front and I faced a [INAUDIBLE] German soldier. He said to me, are you Jewish? And I said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, throw your arms away. What I did-- I didn't do anything. He could shoot me like that. So-- and then I became a prisoner of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were captured by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was captured by the Germans and sent to Germany with all-- all the prisoners of war. And I was not far from Leipzig-- Stalag X or whatever it is. And our work was we had to clean the street in Leipzig. And that lasted for two months, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We had very little food. Although I have to say, we had a guard, an older soldier, German soldier, who-- because the ration was very small, very little. And he brought us to a bakery sometimes to get some more bread or things like that. But it was an old soldier what was in the World War I, maybe. So he knew what it is to be a prisoner of war, I assume. He still had a little-- a little compassion for people with a different situation. But it was hard work. It was the winter, and a very cold winter, because it was in the-- till March. So that whole winter, we had to-- so very bad situation. But taking the situation we were in, it was still very human and very normal. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until, in March 1940, they get an order. All Jewish prisoners of war should be sent back to Poland. Because they had already the plan for the Jews. So they said, why should we keep them as prisoners of war? Let them go back to Poland. We send them to the-- to the concentration camp. So when I came back, my father, with-- with his wife-- You went back to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz, yeah. So my father and-- and my brother, they were not anymore in the home where they lived. They were driven out of the place where they lived with whatever they could carry with them. I hear that from the neighbors and things like that, what had happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- then I got to know-- how, I don't remember how-- I got to know where they went to. So I went there, and they were by Lublin, not far from Lublin. And when I came there, they had a very hard time because he had no official trade. And it was just putting more-- many people in a place where the old people didn't have maybe the work to work there. So I went to a farmer and I worked as a farmer's help. What gave you that idea, to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's probably the only thing you could do. And also, I-- I wanted to be away from the place where they were there, because I heard already they're persecuting Jews there and they're doing a lot of bad things to the Jews. So I figured that gives me-- a farmer, that gives me food, shelter. And a matter of fact, I could even help my pa-- my father, because you got so much paid in form of corn and things like that. I could sell it, and I could help him. What was a really big help for them, too, and for myself. And-- and then my brother went to work there, too-- with another farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also a friend what has-- has worked there, also farmer. So we-- for about two years, I worked there. And I did all the work. I was young, and enthusiastic about work. So-- and-- and you know, what else can you expect? So I worked there for two years, and I did all kinds of-- but of course, a Ukrainian. By Ukrainian, I worked there. They was very nice people. They treated me nice. Had nothing to complain on that, till there came an order that Jews cannot work anymore in the-- any farm there. And all the Jews which worked there has-- have to come to the nearest biggest city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in that time already, my father and his wife, my stepmother, they were sent away already. There was-- they took-- at that time, they took only the older ones. And my-- they were sent away, and I didn't know where they went to. And-- and-- and-- and as I say, when there came the order, that we-- all the Jews had to go to the nearest biggest city. So my brother and myself and a friend what worked also the farmer decided not to go to the city because we expect it could be very bad for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard stories about things being bad? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember in the place where my father and mother lived there that there were-- the Germans came in and they shoot people. They're being buried in the place where they were shot. So we knew already what was going on. There was very much restricted there. You couldn't work there. You couldn't go no place. You couldn't do anything. So we knew it's a lot of restrictions. We didn't imagine it would become worse than that. So we thought always, well, maybe we come through this time. But it seems not, because there were not Jews in around any places-- only the one what works for the farmer, for working on a farmer was really the last thing you're still allowed to work. When these people were called to the city, there was already bad, very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I say, we decided to go in the woods and see what will happen. So we went in the woods. And for three days, we hanged around in the woods, not having any food. The civilian population didn't help us. And-- but we had to have some food. So we heard somehow that in Izbica, another city in Poland, about 20 miles away, something, there are some Jews still left there. So we decided to go there. If there's some Jews, we might get some food, things like that. So when we came there, we-- in the-- in the same evening we went there, some were with other Jews in. And at night, all the Germans came, and all the Jews drove from the houses. So-- so we went hiding in the attics in the hope that the Germans will go, and then come out. But they didn't go. So whoever they picked up, they took them away in a transport. And later, we had to come out because we didn't have any food, didn't have anything to-- different what we can do. So we came out. So all these people what were hidden came out. And they took them all to the train of-- not really to the train. They put them in wagons they had, and they load whoever who couldn't walk on these wagons. And you were among those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among those people. And-- and I was walking. I was younger in that time. And they took us for transport, and it went to Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the transport like? How-- was it a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. We started with wagons. I think we were in trains later, fee-- feed-- cattle trains, cattle wagons, loaded in. I-- I think that's what it was. So anyway, so they brought us to Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea what was happening, why they were doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard rumors that they kill people, but your human sense didn't allow you the idea they're killing people. We-- we thought that it's just rumors. But-- but when I arrived in Sobibor, I realized quickly now what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came with my brother and my friend, came to Sobibor. Now, they-- from the transport-- I don't know how many people there were-- anyway, they picked out about 20 people to work there. And one of the Germans asked you, are you German? Are you Jew? No. He said, where do you come from? I told him I come from Lodz. So he said, out. No questions more, just out. And my brother, they didn't pick out. So I don't know what picking out means because that could have been good or bad, because we still didn't have the idea that really people go to be killed there. When was this, that you got to Sobibor?

speaker: interviewee
text: In November of 1942. So later, when the transport divided, the people which were picked out went to the living quarters. Then we talked with the inmates what's going on. So we immediately hear what's going on, that the people go to the gas chamber. And in the afternoon, they took me to work. And my work, they started to separate the clothes for the people who just went on transport. And when I was doing that, I found the clothes of my brother. And the pictures-- the picture what I have is what I found on my brother's. He had the-- the pictures with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't believe that. I couldn't believe it, but it was so real and so obvious that I couldn't think differently. I couldn't think differently. You know, when you were in Sobibor and you were alert-- not alert enough, if you hear it, you didn't believe it. You didn't believe it is possible that people get killed. That was just so far from-- from normal thinking that I couldn't imagine that it really happened. But if you start to be alert enough of it, you-- you know what's going on. You know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happens to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I started to adjust to routine work, to different kind of work. The food was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work? What was the routine work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly, people had some special work, like we-- I was separating the clothes. That was the most work of a camp. Because you see, that-- our camp was not a-- a working camp. It was a death camp. At this determined hour, everybody that comes here has to be killed. In the beginning, before they picked me out, in a transport before ours, they-- always when a transport came, they picked out about 60, 80 young people to keep when the transport is gone. They cleaned up, I would think, like the luggage away, things like that, and then they shot them. But I guess the Germans didn't find it efficient enough to have every time a new group, so they decided to keep a permanent group working. And that was the work in camp. That was not, as I say, all the kind of work. Now, with this separate-- if you have people, there comes other work. You have shoemakers and-- for the Germans. Mostly it was for the Germans. Shoemakers, claymaker, goldsmith, all the kind of thing. That happened after they kept-- keep people there. And-- but if-- if a transport came, they picked everybody for different kind of work. Like, for example, the hair cutting of the women. That was in a barrack about 10 met-- 10 feet away from the gas chamber. Now, they pick me certain times, also to pick-- just cutting the hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was really a terrible thing. Everything was terrible, but that was especially terrible because there's naked women with children. And they know-- you know they go to the death. And you have to cut-- and you cannot say a word. You cannot say a word because they had the Germans and the guns.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you open your mouth to say something, they just take you with them. So you couldn't say anything. And also, you couldn't help. You couldn't do anything. You could-- either you go with them or you be quiet. It's-- so-- And that was different to my experience at different times. And I-- once in a while they give you 25 with the whip over your behind because you didn't look good or you didn't look right or whatever. It happened. But not reason at all. And then hear somebody got killed for no reason-- shot, I think. There was-- I don't know if someone told you about there was once a-- a group what worked outside the camp, and they killed somebody, a German guard or a Ukrainian guard. And they-- two ran away. And the rest, they got caught. They caught them and brought them back. And as punishment, we had to see always how they were all shot. So-- this one time. Another time happened-- a similar thing happened to that, and that was a not successful-- not uprising or trying something, but it was-- whatever reason. And then they picked-- except the group what they brought back, plus from the regular group what works in camp-- they picked every tenth to go with them to be shot there. And I happened to be in the line like that, number nine. Luck. So-- and then we have to see how they were shot. So those experience was terrible. The food was bad. The treatment was terrible. Only you are tried always-- I always tried not to be obvious, not to see me, if I could, not to fall apart with anything. That whatever they did, just do the minimum and try not-- not to be caught with them. So that maybe helped me a little bit easier, that I didn't have much to do directly with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've come to the end of this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, how many tapes will it be?
